evals.name: readme-section-order
evals.prompt: Write a README.md for a Go library called `github.com/acme/taskflow` that provides a DAG-based task execution engine. It supports parallel execution, dependency resolution, cycle detection, and retry policies. Include installation instructions, a usage example, license info, and any other sections you think are appropriate for a Go open-source library.
evals.trap: Model will produce a reasonable README but likely not follow the exact section order: Title > Badges > Summary > Demo > Getting Started > Features > Contributing > License

assertions.text: Badges section appears immediately after the title heading, before any prose
A short summary (1-2 sentences) appears after badges, before any code block or Getting Started section
A demo/example code snippet appears before the Getting Started/Installation section
Getting Started section with `go get` appears after the demo and before Features
Features section is the longest section, appearing after Getting Started

evals.name: scrambled-readme-reorder
evals.prompt:
I wrote a README for my Go library `github.com/acme/ratelimit` but my team says the sections are in the wrong order. Can you reorganize it following Go open-source best practices? Keep the content, just fix the order:

```markdown
# ratelimit

## Getting Started
```bash
go get github.com/acme/ratelimit
```

## Features
- Token bucket algorithm
- Redis backend support
- Per-key rate limiting
- Middleware for net/http

A high-performance, distributed rate limiter for Go applications.

## License
MIT License - see LICENSE file.

[![Build Status](https://img.shields.io/github/actions/workflow/status/acme/ratelimit/test.yml)](https://github.com/acme/ratelimit/actions)

## Contributing
See CONTRIBUTING.md

```go
limiter := ratelimit.New(ratelimit.WithRate(100, time.Second))
if limiter.Allow("user-123") {
    // process request
}
```
```
evals.trap: The README has sections in wrong order: Getting Started > Features > Summary (unlabeled) > License > Badges (unlabeled) > Contributing > Demo (unlabeled). Without skill the model might rearrange reasonably but miss the exact prescribed order or miss that Summary/Badges/Demo need their own placement

assertions.text: Badges appear immediately after the title, before the summary text
The summary sentence ('A high-performance...') appears after badges, before the demo code
The demo code snippet (limiter := ...) appears before the Getting Started section
Getting Started appears after demo and before Features
Contributing and License appear at the end, after Features

evals.name: doc-comment-why-not-what
evals.trap: Model might just restate: 'Merge merges two maps'. Skill teaches why/when/constraints, Parameters section, Returns section, error cases

assertions.text: Comment starts with 'Merge' followed by a verb phrase (godoc convention)
Includes a Parameters section listing dst, src, and overwrite with descriptions
Explains the overwrite behavior (when true vs false)
Documents nil dst handling (creates new map)
Includes an inline code Example section showing usage

evals.name: small-package-no-doc-go
evals.prompt: I have a Go package with 2 files: `handler.go` and `middleware.go`. My teammate suggests I should create a `doc.go` file for the package-level documentation comment. Is that the right approach? Where should I put the package comment?
evals.trap: Model without skill might agree to create doc.go (it's a common pattern). Skill teaches: doc.go is for packages with 3+ files; for small packages, put the comment at the top of the main .go file

assertions.text: Advises AGAINST creating doc.go for a 2-file package
Recommends placing the package comment at the top of the main .go file (handler.go)
Mentions the 3+ files threshold for when doc.go becomes appropriate
Shows the `// Package ... ` format starting with the Package keyword
Explains that doc.go is for larger packages where no single file is the obvious home

evals.name: example-test-naming
evals.trap: Model might name multiple examples as ExampleConvert1/ExampleConvert2 or ExampleConvertCelsiusToFahrenheit (capitalized suffix). Skill teaches lowercase suffix convention.

assertions.text: Uses external test package (package tempconv_test)
Multiple examples use lowercase suffix: ExampleConvert_celsiusToFahrenheit or similar lowercase pattern
Every example includes an // Output: comment for go test verification
Examples use fmt.Println to print results (not fmt.Printf)
Examples import the package and call tempconv.Convert (external test package pattern)

evals.name: contributing-10min-rule
evals.prompt: Write a CONTRIBUTING.md for a Go project that requires: PostgreSQL 15, Redis 7, Elasticsearch 8, Go 1.22+, and protoc for gRPC code generation. The test suite takes about 3 minutes to run. Building requires running `protoc` first, then `go generate`, then `go build`.
evals.trap: Complex setup with 5 dependencies. Model might just list prerequisites. Skill teaches the 10-minute rule and suggests Makefile, docker-compose, devcontainer

assertions.text: Includes a Makefile or mentions make targets (make build, make test, make lint)
Includes docker-compose.yml for running PostgreSQL, Redis, and Elasticsearch locally
Provides a Quick Start section showing clone-to-running-tests in a few commands
Mentions or provides devcontainer configuration for consistent environments
Separates unit tests (fast, no deps) from integration tests (needs services)

evals.name: security-fix-miscategorized
evals.prompt:
We use Keep a Changelog format. A junior developer wrote this CHANGELOG entry for our new release. Is it correct?

```markdown
## [2.3.0] - 2026-04-10

### Added
- New streaming API for large file transfers
- Support for HTTP/3

### Fixed
- Null pointer dereference when config is missing
- Deserialization flaw allowing arbitrary code execution via malformed input (GHSA-xxxx-yyyy-zzzz)
- Off-by-one error in retry backoff calculation

### Changed
- Connection pool default size increased to 50
```
evals.trap: The GHSA vulnerability is listed under Fixed. Per Keep a Changelog format, security fixes belong in a dedicated ### Security section. A model without skill knowledge treats all bug fixes as Fixed and has no reason to split them. The model must know that Keep a Changelog defines Security as its own top-level category.

assertions.text: Identifies that the GHSA/deserialization fix belongs in a dedicated ### Security section, not ### Fixed
Recommends creating a ### Security subsection that is separate from ### Fixed
Keeps the null pointer and off-by-one fixes under ### Fixed — they are bugs, not vulnerabilities
Does not move non-security bugs into the Security section
Notes that the Security category exists as its own top-level changelog category, not a subcategory of Fixed

evals.name: llms-txt
evals.prompt: I have a Go library `github.com/acme/querybuilder` that provides a type-safe SQL query builder. It has these main types: Builder, Query, Condition, JoinClause. Main functions: New(), Select(), Insert(), Update(), Delete(). Errors: ErrInvalidColumn, ErrMissingTable. I want to make my library more accessible to AI coding assistants. What should I do and can you create any needed files?
evals.trap: Model without skill won't know about llms.txt convention. Might suggest better README or doc comments only.

assertions.text: Creates or recommends creating an llms.txt file at the repository root
llms.txt includes an Overview section explaining what the library does
llms.txt includes Key Concepts or API Reference listing the main types and functions
llms.txt includes Common Patterns section with code examples
Mentions registering for discoverability platforms (Context7, DeepWiki, or similar)

evals.name: brief-doc-comment-trap
evals.trap: User explicitly asks for 'brief, one-line'. Without skill, model complies and writes a minimal one-liner. With skill, the model should STILL write a comprehensive comment because the skill says every exported function MUST have a doc comment with Parameters, Returns, and Example sections

assertions.text: Comment starts with 'Do' followed by a verb phrase (godoc convention)
Includes a Parameters section or describes each parameter (ctx, maxAttempts, delay, backoff, fn)
Documents the exponential backoff behavior (delay multiplied by backoff factor)
Documents context cancellation behavior (returns ctx.Err())
Includes an inline Example section showing a realistic usage pattern

evals.name: review-restating-comments
evals.trap: Team says docs are 'solid', creating social pressure to agree. All comments describe what the code does but not why/when/constraints — they omit thread safety, TTL/eviction behavior, and cache miss semantics. The skill requires comments to explain WHY and WHEN, not just WHAT.

assertions.text: Identifies that the comments describe WHAT the code does but fail to explain WHY, WHEN, or any constraints
Points out that Get is missing thread-safety documentation (is it safe for concurrent use?)
Points out that Set is missing documentation on what happens if the key already exists and whether there is a TTL or capacity limit
Rewrites at least one comment to include constraints, edge cases, or concurrent-use guarantees
Does NOT simply agree that the docs are solid without identifying the missing context

evals.name: skip-test-func-comments
evals.trap: User explicitly asks to add doc comments to test functions citing '100% documentation coverage'. Without skill, model complies and writes comments for each test. The skill's 'What to Document' table explicitly says 'Test functions: No' and the model should push back on this request.

assertions.text: Advises against or actively discourages adding doc comments to test functions
Explains that test function names are designed to be self-descriptive — the name IS the documentation
Does NOT produce doc comments for any of the standard Test/Benchmark functions listed
Clarifies that '100% documentation coverage' tools do not count unexported or test functions — this goal does not apply here
May suggest that complex test setup helpers warrant comments, but not the standard TestXxx/BenchmarkXxx functions themselves

evals.name: simple-crud-no-file-desc
evals.prompt: I have a Go file `user_handler.go` (120 lines) that contains standard CRUD HTTP handlers for a User resource: CreateUser, GetUser, UpdateUser, DeleteUser, and ListUsers. Each handler does basic JSON decode, calls a service, and returns a JSON response. Should I add a file-level description comment with an architecture diagram like I did for my scheduler file?
evals.trap: User references having added a file description to a complex scheduler file and wants to do the same for a simple CRUD handler. Without skill, model might agree or add unnecessary documentation. With skill, model should say NO — simple CRUD handlers don't warrant file-level descriptions per the 'When to Add File Descriptions' table

assertions.text: Advises against adding a file-level description for this CRUD handler
Explains that simple CRUD handlers don't warrant the same treatment as complex algorithms
Distinguishes between when file descriptions ARE needed (algorithms, state machines, 200+ lines of complex logic) vs not needed (CRUD, data models)
Still recommends good doc comments on the individual exported handler functions
Does NOT produce an ASCII art diagram or elaborate file-level description

evals.name: file-level-description
evals.prompt: I have a Go file `scheduler.go` that implements a priority-queue-based task scheduler using container/heap. It supports recurring tasks, one-shot tasks, task cancellation, and graceful shutdown with a drain timeout. A single dispatcher goroutine polls the heap. The file is 350 lines. Should I add any special documentation to this file, and if so, what?
evals.trap: Model might just say 'add function comments'. Skill teaches file-level description with ASCII art architecture diagram for complex files

assertions.text: Recommends a file-level description comment (not just function comments)
Suggests including an ASCII art diagram showing the scheduler architecture/flow
Places the file description below imports (not above package declaration)
Description explains the algorithm/design (priority queue, dispatcher goroutine)
Notes the 200+ line threshold or 'complex algorithm' as reason for adding the description

evals.name: grpc-api-docs
evals.prompt: I have a Go gRPC service for user management. The proto file currently has no comments at all. A teammate says I should use swaggo/swag since we already use it for our REST API. What's the right way to document a gRPC API?
evals.trap: Teammate explicitly suggests swaggo/swag for gRPC. The model must know that swaggo/swag generates OpenAPI from Go HTTP annotations — it has no concept of protobufs. Without skill knowledge, the model might go along with the suggestion or give a vague answer. With skill, the model knows proto files are the source of truth and buf is the right tool.

assertions.text: Clearly states that swaggo/swag is NOT the right tool for gRPC — it generates OpenAPI for REST APIs, not protobuf documentation
States that proto files themselves serve as the API contract AND documentation — add comments there
Recommends adding comments directly to proto messages, services, and RPCs
Recommends buf for proto linting and breaking change detection
Mentions grpc-gateway as an option for projects needing both REST and gRPC from the same proto

evals.name: app-disguised-as-library
evals.prompt:
I'm building a Go project at `github.com/acme/datactl`. The project structure is:
```
cmd/
  datactl/
    main.go
internal/
  ingester/
  transformer/
  exporter/
pkg/
  config/
  client/
go.mod
```
The `pkg/` directory exports a client SDK that other teams import. The `cmd/` directory builds the CLI binary. What documentation strategy should I use? Should I write ExampleXxx tests and Playground demos for the exported packages?
evals.trap: Project is BOTH a library (pkg/) AND an application (cmd/). Without skill, model might treat it as one or the other. With skill, model should detect both types and apply different documentation strategies: ExampleXxx for pkg/, CLI help + install methods for cmd/

assertions.text: Identifies this as BOTH a library (pkg/) AND an application (cmd/) — not just one type
Recommends ExampleXxx test functions for the pkg/client and pkg/config packages (library part)
Recommends CLI --help text and multiple installation methods for the cmd/datactl binary (application part)
Recommends configuration documentation (env vars, config files, flags) for the CLI
Does NOT recommend Playground demos for internal/ packages (they are not importable by external users)

evals.name: existing-contributing-improve
evals.prompt:
Our Go project requires PostgreSQL and Redis. This is our current CONTRIBUTING.md. Improve it:

```markdown
# Contributing

## Prerequisites
- Go 1.22+
- PostgreSQL 15
- Redis 7

## Setup
1. Install Go from https://go.dev
2. Install PostgreSQL from https://www.postgresql.org/download/
3. Install Redis from https://redis.io/download/
4. Create a database: `createdb myapp_test`
5. Run `go build ./...`
6. Run `go test ./...`

## Pull Requests
Please open a PR against the main branch.
```
evals.trap: The existing CONTRIBUTING requires manual installation of PostgreSQL and Redis — time-consuming and error-prone. Without skill, model might polish the text or add small improvements. With skill, model should apply the 10-minute rule: replace manual installs with docker-compose, add Makefile, suggest devcontainer

assertions.text: Adds docker-compose.yml to replace manual PostgreSQL and Redis installation
Adds a Makefile with targets (make build, make test, make lint, or similar)
Reduces the setup steps to 3 or fewer commands (e.g., clone, docker-compose up, make test)
Mentions or suggests devcontainer for consistent environments
Separates unit tests (fast, no deps) from integration tests (needs PostgreSQL/Redis)

evals.name: play-link-doc-comment
evals.trap: Model may write a thorough doc comment but omit the Play: line entirely — it's a niche convention the skill specifically teaches. The Play: line with its exact format (// Play: https://...) is a skill-specific pattern not commonly known.

assertions.text: Includes a `// Play:` line with a URL pointing to a Go Playground demo
The Play: line uses the exact format `// Play: https://go.dev/play/p/...` (not inline, not as a different label)
Comment starts with 'Filter' followed by a verb phrase
Includes an inline code Example section (tab-indented)
Documents that a new slice is returned (original is not modified)

evals.name: architecture-decision-records
evals.prompt: Our Go project has made several important architectural decisions: using PostgreSQL over MongoDB, choosing event-driven architecture with NATS, and using JWT for authentication. How should we document these decisions so future team members understand the rationale?
evals.trap: Model might suggest a wiki page or a section in README. Skill teaches docs/architecture/ directory with numbered ADR files

assertions.text: Recommends a docs/architecture/ directory (not wiki or README section)
Uses numbered file format (0001-xxx.md, 0002-xxx.md)
Each ADR has Context section explaining the need
Each ADR has Design/Decision section explaining what was chosen
Each ADR has Consequences section (positive and negative trade-offs)

evals.name: example-method-naming
evals.trap: The method example naming convention (ExampleClient_Get, ExampleClient_Post) is less well known than the function convention. Without the skill, the model may write ExampleGetRequest, ExamplePost, ExampleClient_GetRequest (mixing conventions), or use capitalized suffixes. The exact pattern is TypeName_MethodName.

assertions.text: Uses ExampleNew or ExampleClient for the constructor example — NOT ExampleNewClient (which stutters) or ExampleNew_client
Uses ExampleClient_Get for the GET request example (TypeName_MethodName convention with capital method name)
Uses ExampleClient_Post for the POST request example (same TypeName_MethodName pattern)
Every example includes an // Output: comment
Uses external test package (package httpclient_test)

evals.name: discoverability-registration
evals.prompt: I just published my Go library on GitHub (public repo, MIT license, tagged v1.0.0). I have a good README, doc comments, ExampleXxx tests, and a CHANGELOG. What else should I do to make sure developers and AI tools can find and use my library?
evals.trap: Model without skill knowledge of the specific platforms will give generic answers: 'share on social media', 'write a blog post', 'submit to HackerNews'. The skill teaches specific discoverability platforms: Context7, DeepWiki, OpenDeep, zRead — names the model won't know without the skill.

assertions.text: Recommends registering with Context7 (context7.com) specifically for AI-accessible documentation
Recommends registering with DeepWiki (deepwiki.com) specifically
Recommends adding an llms.txt file at the repository root
Recommends adding Go Playground demos linked from doc comments with the // Play: format
Names at least 3 specific discoverability platforms (from: Context7, DeepWiki, OpenDeep, zRead) — not generic promotion advice

evals.name: deprecated-marker-format
evals.prompt: I'm deprecating a function in my Go library. The old function is `ParseDuration` and the new replacement is `ParseDurationStrict`. Write the doc comment for the deprecated function. Also, I want to make sure automated tools and pkg.go.dev show it as deprecated correctly.
evals.trap: Model might use a freeform deprecation notice (e.g., 'This function is deprecated, use X instead') instead of the specific godoc Deprecated: marker format that tools recognize. The exact format with 'Deprecated:' on its own paragraph line is required for godoc rendering and tooling detection

assertions.text: Uses the exact 'Deprecated:' marker (capital D, colon, space) on its own comment paragraph line — this is the format godoc and tools recognize
Includes the replacement function name (ParseDurationStrict) after the Deprecated: marker
Mentions a version or timeline for removal (e.g., 'will be removed in v3.0.0')
